Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually carving out time in the mornings and evenings around my other commitments. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time with diagrams, charts, and video content. With about 12 hours a week dedicated to studying, I had to be strategic about what I focused on. I kept a basic schedule written out, though I'll admit some weeks were more chaotic than others depending on what was due.
Q2: My pattern definitely shifted based on deadlines and what was happening in my life. I tried to maintain regularity, but when big assignments were coming up, I'd increase my study time significantly. Some weeks I was very disciplined, logging in daily, and other weeks I'd cluster my work around due dates. The fear of falling behind kept me checking in even when I didn't feel motivated.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements, watching lecture videos, or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just because I felt anxious about missing something important. The fear of being caught off guard by a deadline or a change in the course really drove me to check frequently. I also participated in discussions, so I'd log in to see if anyone had responded to my posts.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became automatic for me to check the LMS first thing when I got access to a computer. Since I don't have internet at home, I'd make it a point to log in whenever I was on campus or at the library. It became part of my routine because I knew I couldn't afford to miss anything. The habit formed out of necessity more than anything else.
Q5: The video content was incredibly valuable to me because I learn best visually. I watched nearly 250 minutes of videos, often pausing and rewinding to make sure I understood concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives clarified things I was confused about. I found that anything with visual explanations or real examples stuck with me much better than plain text readings.
Q6: I'll be honest, I sometimes skipped over lengthy reading materials or supplementary PDFs that seemed less directly connected to exams. If something looked like it wouldn't be tested or wasn't clearly connected to assignments, I'd deprioritize it. With limited time and no internet at home, I had to make tough choices about what to engage with deeply versus what to skim or skip entirely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to my grades or my understanding of core concepts. If I could visualize the payoff—like better exam performance or completing an assignment—I'd invest fully. Activities that seemed vague or disconnected from assessments felt harder to justify, especially when I was already stretched thin with 20 online courses to manage.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. Required work had clear consequences, so I prioritized those even when I was scared or stressed. Optional stuff, like extra readings or bonus discussions, I'd only touch if I had extra time, which was rare. There was always this nagging guilt about not doing them, but survival mode meant focusing on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials when I had access, then study them at home without internet. I also made extensive handwritten notes and diagrams that I'd review repeatedly. My textbooks and printed materials were crucial because I couldn't always be online. A lot of my real learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper, none of which showed up in the system. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts, but that peer interaction wasn't captured digitally. Some of my deepest learning happened when I was sitting alone with my notes, far from any computer.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it if possible, and then work with it offline for days. My LMS might show one view, but I'd have watched that video three or four times on my own device. The system couldn't capture how many times I revisited materials after that initial download.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear and stress made it harder to engage. When assignments piled up or when I felt overwhelmed by the sheer number of courses, I'd freeze a bit. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but there were times when I just felt scared about whether I could keep up. Those moments usually passed, but they were rough while they lasted.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor, especially mid-semester when everything seemed due at once. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan my LMS use carefully, which added another layer of stress. Sometimes I'd be so tired that I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and life stuff definitely affected how present I could be when engaging with course materials.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd watch a video or scroll through materials but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be logged in, technically engaging, but really I was just going through the motions because I was exhausted or worried about other things. Those sessions felt hollow—I knew I wasn't actually learning, just performing the act of studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Sometimes the fear of not doing them well enough paralyzed me, so I'd avoid starting. Other times I was just overwhelmed and needed to prioritize immediate deadlines over longer-term work. My assignment completion rate reflects this—I didn't always finish everything, even though my exam scores show I understood the material.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd structure my entire week around what was due and when. As deadlines approached, my LMS activity would spike dramatically. Without that external pressure, I probably wouldn't have maintained the same level of engagement. The fear of missing deadlines kept me accountable even when motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me, especially when it was encouraging or specific about how to improve. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content. When feedback was delayed or vague, I felt more anxious and uncertain about whether I was on the right track. I appreciated instructors who gave timely responses because it helped calm some of my fears.
Q18: The course structure helped me a lot because it gave me clear milestones to work toward. Having organized modules and predictable patterns made it easier to plan my limited study time. When things were well-structured, I felt less scared about falling behind. The clarity reduced some of my anxiety about what was expected.
Q19: Visual elements and well-organized video content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when materials were text-heavy or when expectations weren't clearly communicated. Also, not having consistent internet access meant I struggled with anything that required real-time participation. The course design worked best when it accommodated different learning styles and access situations.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work, or when they're anxious and checking everything repeatedly without making progress. It might also reflect someone who's engaged with learning but not confident enough to submit their work. Fear of failure can make you very active but also very hesitant to commit to final submissions.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam scores were excellent even though my assignment completion wasn't perfect. I studied a lot offline, so my LMS activity didn't capture everything. Sometimes students who understand the material deeply don't need to be constantly logged in—they're learning in ways the system can't see.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like login frequency, video views, and submission times pretty accurately. But it misses the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and collaborative learning that happens in person. It can't tell you if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. The numbers tell part of the story, not the whole thing.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply what I'm learning, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, and connecting new information to what I already know. For me, engagement also means pushing through fear and showing up even when I'm scared of failing. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with fear and anxiety throughout the course, or how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the peer support I received in person. My resilience in pushing through despite feeling scared—that's invisible in the data but was central to my experience this semester.
